definition: The simulation of human intelligence by machines — enabling systems to learn, reason, and make decisions.

A hypothetical AI system capable of understanding, learning, and applying knowledge across any domain — matching or exceeding human cognitive abilities. Unlike today's narrow AI, AGI would reason broadly and adapt to entirely new situations. AGI does not yet exist.
A subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data and improve over time without being explicitly programmed.
A type of machine learning using neural networks with many layers to model complex patterns in data (images, text, audio).
AI models trained on massive text datasets that can generate, summarize, translate, and reason about human language. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini.
A computing system loosely inspired by the human brain, composed of layers of interconnected nodes that process information.
The branch of AI that enables machines to understand, interpret, and generate human language.
AI systems that create new content — text, images, code, audio, video — rather than just analyzing existing data.
The input or instruction you give to an AI model to get a desired response. The quality of the prompt directly affects output quality.
The practice of designing and refining prompts to get optimal results from AI models — a critical skill for effective AI use.
Providing a few examples in the prompt so the AI understands the pattern and format you expect.
Asking the AI to perform a task without providing any examples — relying on the model's pre-trained knowledge.
A prompting technique that encourages the model to show its reasoning step-by-step, improving accuracy on complex tasks.
A technique that combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval — the model searches a database before generating a response, reducing hallucinations.
Adapting a pre-trained AI model to a specific task or domain by training it further on a specialized dataset.
Using knowledge learned from one task to improve performance on a different but related task — the foundation of modern AI.
When an AI model generates information that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect or fabricated.
The basic units of text that LLMs process. A token can be a word, part of a word, or punctuation. Token limits define how much text a model can handle.
The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) that a model can process in a single conversation. Larger windows = more context awareness.
A parameter that controls randomness in AI output. Low temperature = more predictable/focused. High temperature = more creative/varied.
A numerical representation of text, images, or data in a high-dimensional space — enables AI to understand similarity and relationships.
A database optimized for storing and searching embeddings — essential for RAG, semantic search, and recommendation systems.
The neural network architecture behind modern LLMs. Uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences of data efficiently.
A technique that lets the model focus on the most relevant parts of the input when generating each piece of output.
An AI system that can autonomously plan, reason, use tools, and take actions to accomplish goals — going beyond simple Q&A.
AI systems designed to act independently with minimal human oversight — planning multi-step tasks, calling APIs, and making decisions.
The ability of an AI model to invoke external tools, APIs, or functions to gather data or perform actions beyond text generation.
Architectures where multiple AI agents collaborate, each with specialized roles, to solve complex tasks together.
Safety mechanisms and rules that constrain AI behavior — preventing harmful, biased, or off-topic outputs.
Policies, processes, and controls for responsible AI adoption — covering ethics, compliance, risk, and accountability.
Systematic errors in AI output caused by imbalanced or skewed training data — can lead to unfair or discriminatory results.
The ability to understand and articulate why an AI model made a particular decision — critical for trust and compliance.
Gradual degradation of model performance over time as real-world data patterns shift away from the training data.
The practice of deploying, monitoring, and managing machine learning models in production — DevOps for AI.
The process of using a trained model to make predictions or generate outputs on new data — what happens when you 'use' an AI.
The time it takes for an AI model to generate a response after receiving input. Lower latency = faster responses.
An interface that lets applications interact with AI models programmatically — send prompts, receive responses.
An AI assistant embedded in workflows (coding, writing, analysis) that suggests, completes, or automates tasks in real-time.
A type of generative AI that creates images by gradually removing noise from random data — used by DALL·E, Stable Diffusion, Midjourney.
AI systems that can process and generate multiple types of data — text, images, audio, video — in a single model.
AI models and tools released with open licenses, allowing anyone to use, modify, and build upon them. Examples: Llama, Mistral, Hugging Face.
Artificially generated data used to train AI models when real data is scarce, sensitive, or expensive to collect.
A training technique where human preferences are used to fine-tune AI models — key to making LLMs helpful and safe.
Running AI models locally on devices (phones, sensors, IoT) rather than in the cloud — enabling faster, private, offline inference.
An organizational mindset where AI is a core capability woven into every process, not just an add-on — the future of software delivery.
Developing and deploying AI systems that are fair, transparent, accountable, and aligned with human values.
A security vulnerability where malicious inputs trick an AI into ignoring its instructions or leaking sensitive information.
A large AI model pre-trained on broad data that serves as a base for many downstream tasks. GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, and Llama are all foundation models.
The technique of connecting an AI model's output to verified, factual sources — reducing hallucinations by anchoring responses in real data.
The process of breaking text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) that a language model can process. Different models use different tokenizers.
Hidden instructions given to an AI model that define its personality, rules, and behavior — set by the developer, not visible to the end user.
Breaking a complex task into a series of sequential prompts, where the output of one becomes the input of the next — enabling multi-step reasoning.
The process of coordinating multiple AI components — models, tools, memory, and APIs — into a cohesive workflow or pipeline.
A structured representation of real-world entities and their relationships, used to give AI systems organized, queryable knowledge.
A standardized test or dataset used to evaluate and compare AI model performance — e.g., MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K.
A learnable weight in a neural network. Model size is often expressed in parameters — GPT-4 has trillions, while smaller models have billions.
Specialized processors optimized for AI workloads. GPUs (NVIDIA) and TPUs (Google) accelerate model training and inference by orders of magnitude over CPUs.
A development approach where you describe what you want in natural language and an AI assistant writes the code — making software creation accessible to non-developers.
An open standard that lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources through a universal interface — like USB-C for AI.
The field of AI that enables machines to interpret and understand visual information from images and video — used in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, and more.
The process of tagging training data with correct labels so supervised AI models can learn from examples — a critical step in model development.
An automated workflow that collects, processes, transforms, and feeds data into AI models for training or inference.
The research field focused on ensuring AI systems reliably act in accordance with human intentions and values — especially critical as models become more capable.
AI systems that can operate independently for extended periods, making decisions and taking actions without human approval at each step.
